coredata.dc:title: A novel and high-performance tumor inhibitor of La, N co-doped carbon dots for U251 and LN229 cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Xinyun
Zhong, Huanglian
Liu, Lin
Huang, Jingtao
Xia, Zhuquan
Tang, Zhiji
Wei, Wenjin
Huang, Weilong
Ye, Yuwei
Jiang, Qiuhua
coredata.dc:description: To address the medical challenges posed by glioblastoma, a novel and high-performance tumor inhibitor (La@FA-CDs) composed of folic acid and lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate, was successfully synthesized and demonstrated effectiveness in inhibiting the growth of U251 and LN299 cells. The microstructure of La@FA-CDs was extensively analyzed by FTIR, UV-Vis, XPS, TEM, AFM NMR, and nanoparticle size analyzer. The optical and electrical properties of La@FA-CDs were characterized using a fluorescence spectrometer and a zeta potential analyzer. Biological assays, including the CCK8 proliferation assay, scratch assay, flow cytometry, cytoskeleton staining, and live/dead staining were conducted to assess antitumor properties and cytotoxicity. The result revealed that the La50 %@FA-CDs demonstrated significantly enhanced antitumor activity relative to the undoped sample. Furthermore, the La50 %@FA-CDs demonstrated a dose-dependent cytotoxic effect on two glioblastoma cell lines U251 and LN299. The findings of this study suggested that treatment with La50 %@FA-CDs effectively inhibited migration and proliferation while promoting apoptosis in glioblastoma cells. Meanwhile, the La50 %@FA-CDs showed minimal cytotoxic effects on HEK 293 and HUVEC cells under standard conditions, with only slight toxicity observed in HUVEC cells at high (500 µM) concentrations. These results suggest that La50 %@FA-CDs could be a promising therapeutic agent for glioblastoma treatment, demonstrating both effective inhibition and favorable safety profiles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: La@FA-CDs
Tumor inhibitor
Glioblastoma cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytoskeleton by phalloidin and DAPI staining
In vitro cytotoxicity assessment using CCK-8 assay and cell morphology observation through cytoskeletal staining
Cell apoptosis by live/dead staining and Annexin-V/PI staining
CCK8 assay and live/dead staining for in vitro biocompatibility

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was sponsored by Jiangxi Provincial Health Technology Project; Science and Technology Department of Jiangxi Province (Jiangxi Natural Science Foundation, No. 20224BAB206069, No. 20203BBGL73174, and No. 20244BAB28020); Jiangxi Province High-level and High-skilled Leading Talents Training Project (2021); Ganzhou Science and Technology Plan Project (2023LNS17423 and GZ2024YLJ004).

This study was Sponsored by Jiangxi Provincial Health Technology Project; Science and Technology Department of Jiangxi Province (Jiangxi Natural Science Foundation, No. 20224BAB206069, No. 20203BBGL73174, and No. 20244BAB28020); Jiangxi Province High-level and High-skilled Leading Talents Training Project (2021); Ganzhou Science and Technology Plan Project (2023LNS17423).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR; (b) UV-Vis; (c) FA-CDs-C1s; (d) FA-CDs-N1s; (e) FA-CDs-O1s; (f) La@FA-CDs-C1s; (g) La@FA-CDs-N1s; (h) La@FA-CDs-O1s; (i) La@FA-CDs-La2p3/2.
TEM, grain size, AFM, and height of FA-CDs (a, c, e, g, i) and La50 %@FA-CDs (b, d, f, h, j) inhibitors.

Fluorescence spectra and zeta potential of inhibitors under different conditions: (a) different La ratios, (b) normalized spectra at different La ratios, (c) different pH values, (d) different times, (e) different emission wavelength, (f) zeta potential.

La50 %@FA-CDs inhibited the migration of glioblastoma cells. (a, b) CCK-8 detection of U251 and LN229 after FA-CDs treatment. (c, d) CCK-8 detection of U251 and LN229 after La50 %@FA-CDs treatment. (e, f) CCK-8 detection of U251 and LN229 after TMZ treatment. TMZ: Temozolomide. Data are presented as the means ± SEM. One-way ANOVA was used for statistical analyses shown in this figure (* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001; ns, not significant). Number of experiments: n ≥ 3.

La50 %@FA-CDs promoted apoptosis in U251 and LN229 cells. (a) Live/dead cell staining. Green cells represent living cells, and red cells represent dead cells. Scale bar 50 µm. (b) The apoptotic cells were measured by flow cytometry with Annexin V and PI double staining. (c, d) Statistical quantification of (a). (e, f) Statistical quantification of (b). Data are presented as the means ± SEM. One-way ANOVA was used for statistical analyses shown in this figure (* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001; ns, not significant). Number of experiments: n ≥ 3.

La50 %@FA-CDs inhibits the migration of glioblastoma cells. (a) Scratch assay to evaluate the migration of U251 cells. Scale bar 100 µm. (b) The cell migration assay was performed to examine the alteration in the number of migrating cells in LN229 cells. Scale bar 100 µm (c) Statistical quantification of (a). (d) Statistical quantification of (b). Data are presented as the means ± SEM. One-way ANOVA was used for statistical analyses shown in this figure (* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01; ns, not significant). Number of experiments: n ≥ 3.

Biocompatibility of the La50 %@FA-CDs. (a) CCK-8 cell proliferation assay of the HUVEC cells. (b) CCK-8 cell proliferation assay of the HEK 293 cells. (c) Live/dead cell staining. Green cells denote living cells while red cells indicate dead cells. Scale bar 50 µm. Data are presented as the means ± SEM. One-way ANOVA was used for statistical analyses shown in this figure (* p < 0.05, ns, not significant). Number of experiments: n ≥ 3.

head.ce:title: A novel and high-performance tumor inhibitor of La, N co-doped carbon dots for U251 and LN229 cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, 341000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the medical challenges posed by glioblastoma, a novel and high-performance tumor inhibitor (La@FA-CDs) composed of folic acid and lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate, was successfully synthesized and demonstrated effectiveness in inhibiting the growth of U251 and LN299 cells. The microstructure of La@FA-CDs was extensively analyzed by FTIR, UV-Vis, XPS, TEM, AFM NMR, and nanoparticle size analyzer. The optical and electrical properties of La@FA-CDs were characterized using a fluorescence spectrometer and a zeta potential analyzer. Biological assays, including the CCK8 proliferation assay, scratch assay, flow cytometry, cytoskeleton staining, and live/dead staining were conducted to assess antitumor properties and cytotoxicity. The result revealed that the La50 %@FA-CDs demonstrated significantly enhanced antitumor activity relative to the undoped sample. Furthermore, the La50 %@FA-CDs demonstrated a dose-dependent cytotoxic effect on two glioblastoma cell lines U251 and LN299. The findings of this study suggested that treatment with La50 %@FA-CDs effectively inhibited migration and proliferation while promoting apoptosis in glioblastoma cells. Meanwhile, the La50 %@FA-CDs showed minimal cytotoxic effects on HEK 293 and HUVEC cells under standard conditions, with only slight toxicity observed in HUVEC cells at high (500 µM) concentrations. These results suggest that La50 %@FA-CDs could be a promising therapeutic agent for glioblastoma treatment, demonstrating both effective inhibition and favorable safety profiles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Glioblastoma, the leading type of primary malignant brain tumor, poses a significant threat to human health due to its high mortality rate, short survival duration, and poor prognosis [1–3]. Statistics show that the 2-year survival rate for glioblastoma patients is just 27.2 %, and the 5-year survival rate falls below 10 % [4]. The effective treatment of glioblastoma remains to be a major challenge in medical research. Currently, clinical treatment for glioblastoma primarily involves three approaches: chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and surgical treatment [5]. Surgical treatment offers the advantage of providing a direct and rapid effect. In early-stage patients, surgery has the potential to provide a complete cure; however, it is less effective for those with intermediate to advanced stages of cancer. Moreover, the significant trauma caused by surgery can decrease the patient’s immune system and lead to various complications. Radiation therapy kills tumor tissue through radiation, but it can also cause significant harm to the human body. Chemotherapy involves the use of specific drugs to target and destroy tumor tissue, thereby achieving therapeutic effects. Currently, it has become one of the important means of comprehensive treatment for tumors. However, chemotherapy is limited by the non-specific distribution of drugs and side effects, including drug resistance from overuse, which reduces its overall effectiveness. Therefore, developing novel and high-performance drugs against glioblastoma has become an urgent challenge, requiring solutions from the fields of materials science, chemical engineering, and biomedical engineering.

The advancement of technology, particularly the emergence of nanotechnology, has revolutionized the development of anti-tumor materials leading to significant breakthroughs in cancer treatment and demonstrated promising potential for widespread application [6–8]. However, the blood-brain barrier (BBB) often prevents anti-tumor materials from reaching the brain parenchyma, which is a major obstacle limiting the effectiveness of chemotherapeutic agents in targeting brain tumors. Therefore, novel anti-tumor materials must exhibit a strong ability to penetrate the BBB. Carbon dots (CDs), a type of nanomaterial, demonstrate low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, good water solubility, and improved capability for BBB penetration [9–14]. For instance, Yang et al. [15] developed CDs using two endogenous nutrients, d-glucose and l-tryptophan, as precursors. The resulting CDs were shown to effectively cross the BBB and exhibit multi-emission fluorescence. Zhang et al. [16] investigated the effectiveness of two types of CDs in targeting tau aggregation and found that their surface diversity and small particle size enabled the CDs to effectively cross the BBB. Seven et al. [17] prepared CDs from glucose and evaluated their ability to cross the BBB using both rat and zebrafish models. The results showed that the obtained CDs could cross the BBB via glucose transporters and likely accumulate in neurons.

Moreover, CDs with remarkable optical properties, surface activity, and anti-tumor effects can be used as fluorescent probes, drug carriers, and tumor inhibitors [18]. Galan et al. [19,20] successfully synthesized a series of functionalized CDs with outstanding fluorescence properties, which can be utilized as fluorescent probes for glioblastoma detection. Algarra et al. [21] synthesized CDs from 2-acrylamido-2-methylpropanesulfonic acid and used as a carrier for the drug riluzole. Analysis revealed that the synthesized CDs could effectively deliver riluzole to glioblastoma cells, resulting in successful treatment. Hettiarachchi et al. [22] used CDs as drug carriers and combined transferrin with two anticancer drugs, epirubicin and temozolomide, to develop a triple conjugated system. The findings demonstrated that this triple conjugated system demonstrated higher cytotoxicity toward glioblastoma cell lines than other systems. Sun et al. [23] synthesized functional CDs with self-targeting using d-glucose and l-aspartic acid as raw materials. The targeting inhibition function of CDs was attributed to the formation of RGD-like functional groups on the edges of the CDs during synthesis, which enhanced their targeting efficiency for glioblastoma cells. Leblanc et al. [24] developed water-soluble CDs using indocyanine green, showing remarkable imaging ability and targeting selectivity for glioblastoma stem cells. In vivo experiments displayed that these carbon dots could function as a photothermal therapeutic agent, demonstrating significant inhibitory effects on tumor tissues in tumor-bearing mice.

Folic acid (FA), an effective tumor-targeting agent that binds to overexpressed folate receptors at tumor sites, has limited therapeutic potential due to its poor water solubility. The good water dispersibility of CDs allows for the effective use of FA to prepare CDs, enhancing their ability to exert anti-tumor effects in living organisms. Xia et al. [25] synthesized FA-based carbon dots (FA-CDs) using FA and copper gluconate as raw materials. They found that the resulting FA-CDs could reduce the conversion of H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (·OH) within cells and effectively deplete glutathione (GSH) in tumor cells, thereby inhibiting tumor growth. Tong et al. [26] prepared FA-CDs using a one-step hydrothermal method with nitrogen-containing folic acid and L-arginine precursors. They demonstrated that the FA-CDs exhibited remarkable luminescence, excellent biocompatibility, and effective targeting of cancer cells. Nasrin et al. [27] utilized the combination of curcumin and FA to prepare novel FA-CDs and enhance the efficacy of photodynamic therapy (PDT) in cancer cells. The result found that FA-CDs interacted with cancer cells through folate receptor-mediated pathways, resulting in significant localization in the nucleus and ultimately enhancing PDT efficiency.

Rare earth elements have a special electronic layer structure, which can effectively absorb and emit light of specific wavelengths, allowing them to combine with other types of materials to form new optical functional materials with a wide range of properties and applications. Moreover, certain rare earth ions display excellent stability and the ability to bind with biomolecules, paving the way for innovative approaches in drug design [28,29]. In recent years, complexes of various rare earth metals have shown significant antitumor activity in tumor-bearing animal models. For instance, Shi et al. [30] synthesized Cr3 + and Eu3+ co-doped rare earth nanomaterials using a combustion method, followed by surface functionalization to enhance their targeted anti-cancer capabilities. The in vivo experiments revealed that the nanomaterials effectively inhibited the growth of in-situ subcutaneous lymphoma in mice. Tao et al. [31] developed a nanomaterial system composed of rare earth nanoparticles conjugated with dihydroartemisinic acid and targeted antibodies. The study demonstrated that PDT for tongue cancer was effectively achieved through the photothermal effects of the rare earth elements Yb/Er/Eu. Chen et al. [32] successfully constructed a tumor microenvironment responsive nanomedicine through the self-assembly of rare earth elements Eu/Gd/Er and biomolecule nucleotides. Combined with the photothermal activity of Eu/Gd/Er, a precise treatment of tumors was achieved. However, research on the use of these rare earth elements for inhibiting glioblastoma remains in its early stages.

Lanthanum (La), a typical representative of rare earth elements, presents a good anti-tumor ability due to its unique ƒ-electropositive configuration, as confirmed by previous studies [33,34]. Compared with other rare earth elements, La offers two distinct advantages, making it a promising material for treating glioblastoma. Firstly, previous studies have revealed that glioblastoma cells naturally tend to accumulate La and its free La³ ⁺ ions in the cytoplasm more efficiently than normal astrocytes [35]. Second, La can form stable lanthanum oxide, which can be used to design nanocomposites and applied to bypass the BBB [36].

In this study, a new inhibitor material (La@FA-CDs) for glioblastoma treatment was developed by integrating the distinct properties of La-CDs with the therapeutic potential of the rare earth element La. FTIR, UV-Vis, XPS, AFM, TEM, nanoparticle size, and zeta potential analyzer were used to analyze the chemical structure, surface morphology, grain size, and zeta potential of La@FA-CDs. A fluorescence spectrometer was used to analyze the optical behavior of La@FA-CDs. Finally, a series of biological experiments including CCK8 proliferation assay, scratch assay, flow cytometry, cytoskeleton staining, and live/dead staining demonstrated the outstanding antitumor properties and biocompatibility of the material.

The folic acid and lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate were purchased from Jiangsu Ruifeng Biochemistry Co., Ltd while distilled water was obtained from our laboratory. The high-pressure reactor was purchased from Xi'an Hongchen Instrument Factory. The human glioblastoma cell lines U251 and LN299 were acquired from Wuhan Priella Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Wuhan, China). CCK-8 was purchased from Dojindo Laboratories (Japan). Live & Dead Viability Kit and Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis detection kit were purchased from Nanjing KeyGen Biotech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Temozolomide (TMZ) was obtained from Selleck Chemicals (Texas, USA).

The synthesis process of La@FA-CDs is shown in Fig. 1. Initially, precise amounts of folic acid and lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate were measured using a precision electronic scale, based on the experimental design, with mass ratios of 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, and 50 %. After that, the measured powders were dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water and then transferred to the ultrasonic equipment to accelerate dissolution. Once fully dissolved, the solution was transferred to a high-pressure reactor and heated in a blast drying oven at 180 °C for 8 h. When the reaction vessel cooled to room temperature, impurities in the reaction solution were removed through vacuum filtration. It was then centrifuged at 15,000 r/min to eliminate large, poorly soluble particles, with the pellet discarded and the supernatant retained for further use. The solution was dialyzed for 72 h using a 1000 Da molecular weight cut-off dialysis bag, with the distilled water replaced every 12 h. Finally, the obtained solution was transferred to a vacuum drying oven for drying treatment at a temperature of 85 °C for 48 h. For simplicity, the folic acids with different mass ratios of lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate (0, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %) were abbreviated as FA-CDs, La10 %@FA-CDs, La20 %@FA-CDs, La30 %@FA-CDs, La50 %@FA-CDs, respectively.

The functional group and elemental state of FA-CDs and La@FA-CDs were confirmed via FTIR, UV-Vis, XPS, and NMR. The micro-morphology, distribution, size, and zeta potential of the prepared FA-CDs and La@FA-CDs were analyzed using TEM, AFM, nanoparticle size, and zeta potential analyzer.

The human glioblastoma cell lines U251, LN299, HUVEC, and HEK 293 were acquired from Wuhan Priella Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Cells were grown in DMEM (Biological Industries, Kibbutz Beit Haemek, Israel) supplemented with 10 % FBS (Biological Industries, Kibbutz Beit Haemek, Israel) and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) at 37℃ under 5 % CO2. To evaluate the anti-glioma efficacy, glioma cells were treated with various concentrations (0 %, 10 %, 30 %, 50 %) of lanthanum-doped FA-CDs for 24 h. Among these concentrations, 300 µM La50 %@FA-CDs (LD50) was selected for subsequent experiments. Control groups were treated with temozolomide (TMZ) or PBS. Cell viability, scratch assay, cytoskeleton staining, and live/dead staining were performed 24 h after treatment, with three wells used for each group. Three wells were used for each group. Experiments were repeated at least 3 times.

Cell viability was assessed using CCK-8 assay (CK04, Dojindo, Kumamoto, Japan) as previously described [37]. In brief, glioblastoma cell lines (U251, LN229) and normal mammalian cell lines (HUVEC, HEK 293) were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 6000 cells/well in 100 µL culture medium and incubated overnight. Cells were treated with or without 300 µM La50 %@FA-CDs for 24 h. After treatment, the cells were washed with pre-warmed PBS (37°C), and then 10 µL of CCK-8 reagent was added to each well containing 100 µL of culture medium. The plates were then incubated for 2 h in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere, and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader. Cell viability was represented as a percent of the control (untreated cells).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytoskeleton by phalloidin and DAPI staining
ce:section.ce:para: Cytoskeleton staining followed by nucleus staining was performed by incubating cells sequentially with Alexa Fluor 546–conjugated phalloidin (Sigma Aldrich; USA) and DAPI (Solaibao Life Science Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Briefly, approximately 3000 cells were seeded in 24-well plates with complete DMEM and allowed to form a confluent monolayer for 10–12 h in a CO2 incubator. The cells were then treated with La50 %@FA-CDs for 24 h before cytoskeleton staining. Finally, cells were washed with PBS and stained with phalloidin (10 μg/mL) and DAPI (1 μg/mL) for 10 min. After incubation, PBS was used to wash away the excess dye. Finally, the images of labeled cells were acquired with a Zeiss LSM 880 confocal microscope. Images of LN229 cells, obtained with two different filters, were merged to visualize the position and morphology of the cell’s nucleus and cytoskeleton.

Cell apoptosis analysis was conducted using an Annexin V- FITC/PI kit (KeyGen Biotech, Nanjing, China) [38]. Briefly, after treating the cells with 300 µM La50 %@FA-CDs for 24 h, the cells were collected, washed twice with ice-cold PBS, and centrifuged at 800 ×g for 5 min. The cells were then resuspended in 500 μL of binding buffer containing 5 μL of Annexin V and 5 μL of PI and incubated for 10 min at room temperature. The samples were analyzed using a BD FACS Canto Ⅱ flow cytometer (BD Biosciences) and the average percentage of apoptotic cells was calculated from three independent cell cultures.

Glioblastoma and normal mammalian cell lines were treated with 300 µM La50 %@FA-CDs or FA-CDs, and live/dead staining was performed 24 h post-treatment using the Live/Dead Assay (KeyGEN BioTECH, Nanjing, China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions [39]. The staining solution, consisting of 2 µM calcein AM and 8 µM PI in PBS, was added to the cells and incubated for 30 minutes. The stained samples were then observed using a fluorescence microscope (Leica DM18, Germany) with a 20x objective lens. The experiment was carried out at least in triplicate using independent cell cultures.

A scratch assay was performed to assess the migratory capacity of cells following 24-h treatment with La50 %@FA-CDs or FA-CDs [40]. Briefly, approximately 0.2 × 106 cells were seeded in 24-well plates containing complete DMEM and incubated for 10–12 h in a CO2 incubator to form a confluent monolayer. A scratch was created using a sterile 10 µL pipette tip, and the cells were subsequently washed with sterile PBS to remove dislodged or non-adherent cells. The cells were then treated with DMEM media containing 1 % FBS and 300 µM La50 %@FA-CDs, and microphotographs were recorded at 0 and 24 h post-scratching using a light microscope. The percentage of cell migration was quantified by determining the area of the cell-free zone immediately after scratching at 0 and 24 h using ImageJ (NIH Image J system, Bethesda, MD).
All experimental data were repeated three times and expressed as Mean ± SEM. Data analysis was performed using One way-ANOVA with a p < 0.05 being considered statistically significant.

Fig. 2 shows the FTIR, UV-Vis, and XPS spectra of FA-CDs and La@FA-CDs. The FTIR spectrum of the raw materials (La(NO3)3·6 H2O and FA) is presented in Fig. S1 . After reaction, a large number of absorption peaks were observed in the FA-CDs (Fig. 2a), primarily corresponding to C-OH, C-C, CC, CN, CO, N-H, C-H, -CH3, O-H at 1184.5, 1380, 1580, 1650, 1696, 1587, 2830.7, 2993.6, 3408.7 cm−1 [41,42]. Compared with FA, the absorption peaks of C-C and CC became more pronounced, suggesting significant hybridization of sp² carbons in FA. After incorporating La, new peaks appeared, with the one at 1042 cm⁻¹ corresponding to the vibration of La-OH, while the peak at 1394 cm⁻¹ attributed to the stretching vibration of NO₃⁻ from residual La(NO₃)₃·6H₂O [43]. Meanwhile, the peak at 755–751 cm⁻¹ corresponded to the La-H bond, indicating successful doping of the La atom [44]. In the UV-Vis spectrum, the absorption peaks of La(NO3)3·6 H2O and FA were located at 310 and 294 nm, respectively (Fig. S2). After the formation of FA-CDs (Fig. 2b), an absorption peak was observed at 332 nm, likely corresponding to the n-π* electronic transition of the -CN bond. However, the intensity of the absorption peak in La@FA-CDs was reduced, suggesting that the electronic structure was altered in the presence of La atoms. Moreover, as the La ratio increased, the intensity of the absorption peak initially decreased and then increased.

The C1s XPS spectrum of FA-CDs (Fig. 2c) displayed four distinct peaks at binding energies of 284.5, 285.6, 287.6, and 288.5 eV, corresponding to C-C/CC, C-O/CN, C-N, and CO bonds, respectively [45]. Meanwhile, the N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d) of FA-CDs can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 398.2 and 399.7 eV, which corresponded to N-H and N-C bonds [46]. Moreover, the O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2e) exhibited two characteristic peaks at 531 eV and 532.7 eV, corresponding to O-C and OC bonds, respectively [47]. After the doping with La, La50 %@FA-CDs was selected as a representative analysis similarity observed in the XPS spectra across La@FA-CDs samples. The C1s and O1s XPS spectra of La50 %@FA-CDs showed no significant changes (Fig. 2f and h). However, a new satellite peak was observed in the N1s spectrum of La50 %@FA-CDs (Fig. 2g). The La2p3/2 XPS spectrum showed two peaks at 833.5 eV and 837.2 eV (Fig. 2i), indicating the presence of La in the + 3 and + 4 oxidation state in La50 %@FA-CDs. Further, the 1H NMR of FA-CDs and La50 %@FA-CDs are shown in Fig. S3, providing further evidence of the successful synthesis of La@FA-CDs.

The morphology and size of FA-CDs and La50 %@FA-CDs were analyzed using TEM and observed results are presented in Fig. 3a-d, showing the dispersion of a large number of FA-CDs nanoparticles within the range of 2.5–5.5 nm. Meanwhile, the majority of FA-CDs particles exhibited a spherical shape with uniform size distribution and high dispersion, indicating good dispersion in distilled water (Fig. 3a and c). Analysis revealed that the primary size of FA-CDs ranged between 3.5 and 4.0 nm. After doping the La atom, the size distribution of La50 %@FA-CDs expanded to 2.5–6.5 nm, with a predominant size of 4.0–6.0 nm, which was larger than that of FA-CDs (Fig. 3b and d). Furthermore, the particle size analysis from the nanoparticle size analyzer (Fig. S4) showed an upward trend as the La ratio increased from 0 % to 50 %, suggesting that La doping contributed to an expanded inhibitor structure.

The distribution and height of FA-CDs and La50 %@FA-CDs are analyzed by AFM (Fig. 3e-j). For FA-CDs, numerous nanoparticles were visible on the surface of the Si wafer (Fig. 3e). Moreover, the FA-CDs inhibitor demonstrated uniform dispersion without clear aggregation, consistent with the observations from TEM. From a three-dimensional perspective, the height of FA-CDs nanoparticles was found to be below 5 nm (Fig. 3g). More specifically, local measurements indicated that the height of FA-CDs ranged from 1.2 to 3.5 nm (Fig. 3g). However, after doping La atom, there was no significant change in the distribution status, but the height of La50 %@FA-CDs was increased significantly (Fig. 3f and h). Measurements revealed that the height distribution of La50 %@FA-CDs ranged from 1.5 to 12 nm (Fig. 3j).

The fluorescence spectra of FA-CDs and La@FA-CDs under different conditions are shown in Fig. 4 while the fluorescence spectra of raw material (La(NO3)3·6 H2O and FA) are presented in Fig. S5. Obviously, the fluorescence peaks of La(NO3)3·6 H2O and FA was around 451 and 529 nm, respectively. With varying La ratios (Fig. 4a), the fluorescence intensity of La@FA-CDs initially decreased and then increased, with the lowest intensity observed at La20 %@FA-CDs. Among the samples, only the fluorescence intensity of La50 %@FA-CDs was higher than that of FA-CDs. Furthermore, after doping with La, the fluorescence peak shifted to a longer wavelength, indicating that the La ratio played a crucial role in modulating the luminescent properties of La@FA-CDs. Moreover, to further investigate the dependence of La on fluorescence spectra, the normalized fluorescence spectrum of La@FA-CDs is presented in Fig. 4b. Analysis revealed that the peaks of the normalized fluorescence spectra for FA-CDs, La10 %@FA-CDs, La20 %@FA-CDs, La30 %@FA-CDs, La50 %@FA-CDs were at approximately 458, 462, 465, 465, and 469 nm, respectively, indicating a red shift in the fluorescence spectrum upon the addition of La. Meanwhile, the full width at half-maximum of FA-CDs, La10 %@FA-CDs, La20 %@FA-CDs, La30 %@FA-CDs, La50 %@FA-CDs was about 89, 92, 92, 104 and 91 nm, respectively. These changes are closely associated with the increase in the size of the inhibitors following the addition of La [48,49].

As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence behavior of La50 %@FA-CDs underwent significant changes with varying pH values in the testing environment, particularly affecting both the fluorescence intensity and the peak position. As the pH value increased from 1 to 14, the fluorescence intensity of La50 %@FA-CDs initially increased and then decreased, reaching a maximum at pH 7. Meanwhile, a comparison revealed that the fluorescence intensity of La50 %@FA-CDs was significantly higher in acidic environments than in alkaline environments. Moreover, the peak position demonstrated an overall trend of initially shifting negatively, followed by a positive shift. For blending time (Fig. 4d), the La50 %@FA-CDs exhibited different luminescence intensities at different blending times, while the peak positions remained relatively. Comparative analysis revealed that the luminescence intensity of La50 %@FA-CDs was highest at a blending time of 1 h and lowest at 5 h, indicating that blending time was a crucial factor influencing the luminescence of La50 %@FA-CDs. Regarding the emission wavelength (Fig. 4e), with the wavelength increased from 300 to 390 nm, the fluorescence intensity of La50 %@FA-CDs displayed an increasing trend. Meanwhile, the fluorescence peak shifted towards the left, demonstrating that variations in the emission wavelength influenced the luminescent behavior of La50 %@FA-CDs. The zeta potential of all inhibitors is shown in Fig. 4f with the detailed potential distribution map provided in Fig. S6. With the increase of La ratio, the zeta potential initially increased and then decreased, reaching its highest value at La30 %@FA-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assessment using CCK-8 assay and cell morphology observation through cytoskeletal staining
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the anti-glioblastoma properties of the newly synthesized carbon dot materials, the CCK8 assay was used to assess their effects on the proliferation of glioblastoma cells [50]. The cytotoxic effects of FA-CDs and La@FA-CDs on U251 and LN229 glioblastoma cell lines were evaluated using the CCK-8 assay. U251 and LN229 cells were treated with gradient concentrations of FA-CDs for 24 h, showing a dose-dependent cytotoxicity, with cell viability decreasing to approximately 20 % in U251 cells and 40 % in LN229 cells at a concentration of 900 μM ( Fig. 5a and b). Subsequently, to further investigate the potential enhancement of the anti-glioma effect by La@FA-CDs, glioma cells were treated with La-doped FA-CDs at various concentrations and La doping ratios for 24 h. Conversely, 300 µM La50 %@FA-CDs (LD50) demonstrated a more pronounced cytotoxic effect on both U251 and LN229 cell lines (Fig. S7 and Fig. 5c, d). Therefore, we ultimately selected this concentration and lanthanum doping ratio of La@FA-CDs, which has the lowest IC50 value, for the subsequent experiments. For comparison, Temozolomide (TMZ) was used as a chemotherapeutic agent [51–53] and the results demonstrated an IC50 value of approximately 200 μM (Fig. 5e and f). Thus, the concentration of 200 μM was selected for the following experiment. In summary, the La50 %@FA-CDs demonstrated a stronger cytotoxic effect on glioblastoma cell lines U251 and LN229 compared to FA-CDs, with the effect observed at lower concentrations. The effectiveness of La50 %@FA-CDs in reducing cell viability was comparable to or even higher than that of TMZ.

Previous studies revealed that tumor cells undergo morphological changes after drug treatment, including cell shrinkage and eventual death [54–56]. To investigate the effects of carbon dot materials on glioblastoma cell lines LN229, the phalloidin (red) staining F-actin was used to highlight the cytoskeleton, while DAPI (blue) was used to stain the nuclei. When the cells were treated with 300 µM La50 %@FA-CDs for 24 h, clear morphological changes were observed compared to the control group (Fig. S8). For comparison, cells treated with the chemotherapeutic agent TMZ exhibited significant cytoskeletal disruption, similar to the effects observed with La50 %@FA-CDs. These results implied that the La50 %@FA-CDs could exhibit a potent cytotoxic effect against glioblastoma cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell apoptosis by live/dead staining and Annexin-V/PI staining
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the antitumor effects of La50 %@FA-CDs on glioblastoma cell lines, live/dead staining, and Annexin V/PI staining assays were conducted. The control groups for both U251 and LN229 cells displayed high viability with minimal cell death (green indicating live cells and red indicating dead cells), while the La50 %@FA-CDs treatment significantly increased the number of dead cells, demonstrating a more pronounced effect ( Fig. 6a, c, d). The chemotherapeutic agent TMZ induced extensive cell death, similar to the effects observed with La50 %@FA-CDs. To further verify whether La50 %@FA-CDs promoted cell death through apoptosis, Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining was carried out (Fig. 6b, e, f). The findings indicated that the La50 %@FA-CDs resulted in a significant increase in both early and late apoptotic cells, demonstrating a strong pro-apoptotic effect similar to TMZ. These findings collectively suggest that La50 %@FA-CDs exhibit potent cytotoxic effects on glioblastoma cells by promoting apoptosis, as evidenced by both live/dead staining and Annexin V/PI staining assays.

To evaluate the effects of carbon dot materials on cell migration, scratch wound healing assays were performed on U251 and LN229 glioblastoma cell lines [57,58]. The experimental results showed that La50 %@FA-CDs significantly affected the cell migration in U251 and LN229 glioblastoma cell lines ( Fig. 7a and c). In the control group, cells displayed significant migration after 24 h, while FA-CDs treatment slightly hindered cell movement. However, La50 %@FA-CDs treatment significantly inhibited cell migration, with minimal wound closure observed, similar to the effect of TMZ. Quantitative analysis revealed that La50 %@FA-CDs significantly reduced the migration distance compared to FA-CDs, highlighting the improved efficacy (Fig. 7b and d). These results suggest that La50 %@FA-CDs effectively inhibit glioblastoma cell migration, highlighting their potential as a therapeutic strategy to prevent tumor metastasis. A current study demonstrated that La50 %@FA-CDs can modulate the cytoskeletal structure of LN229 cells, potentially playing a role in the suppression of glioma cell migration. The observed effect may also be associated with changes in gene expression and signaling pathways, necessitating further exploration in future research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CCK8 assay and live/dead staining for in vitro biocompatibility
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluating the toxicity profile of La50 %@FA-CDs is essential for assessing its potential as a viable therapeutic agent for human use. Since HUVEC and HEK 293 are human-derived cell lines commonly used for toxicology and pharmacology studies, we used these two cell lines to test the impact of La50 %@FA-CDs, and the effects were evaluated using CCK-8 and live/dead staining assays. Given that 300 µM La50 %@FA-CDs showed promising anti-glioma effects in previous studies, concentrations around this value were selected for toxicity evaluation in HUVEC and HEK 293 cells. The results revealed that La50 %@FA-CDs displayed minimal cytotoxicity on both HUVEC and HEK 293 cells across a range of concentrations (0–500 μM) ( Fig. 8a and b). There was no significant reduction in cell viability at most concentrations, with only a slight decrease observed at the highest concentration of 500 μM in HUVEC cells (*p < 0.05) (Fig. 8a), indicating that the La50 %@FA-CDs exhibited minimal adverse effects on normal mammalian cells. This observation was further supported by live/dead staining images, where the HEK 293 and HUVEC cells predominantly stained green (indicating live cells) even at higher concentrations of La50 %@FA-CDs (Fig. 8c). Only a small number of red-stained dead cells were observed, further emphasizing the higher safety profile of La50 %@FA-CDs across the various dosages tested in normal mammalian cells lines. Furthermore, while previous studies indicated the strong tumor-targeting ability and favorable BBB permeability of carbon dot materials, temozolomide, a standard clinical anti-glioma drug, was used as a control in this study. However, several limitations remain in the current study. For example, it is unclear whether the carbon dot material used here demonstrates improved targeting capability and more pronounced anti-tumor effects compared to other materials. Furthermore, while the in vitro results indicated that La50 %@FA-CDs showed strong anti-glioma activity, their capacity to penetrate the BBB in vivo is yet to be determined. Future investigations will be required to further address these concerns and further evaluate their therapeutic potential.

CDs are innovative and eco-friendly materials with broad applications, particularly in imaging. In recent years, they have garnered significant attention for their potential in antitumor research. In the current study, the N and La co-doped CDs with varying La ratios were synthesized to investigate their antitumor properties. The analysis revealed that the La atom predominantly existed in the + 3 and + 4 oxidation states within La@FA-CDs. Moreover, the size distribution of FA-CDs increased significantly following the doping of La. Meanwhile, the La50 %@FA-CDs exhibited improved glioblastoma tumor-killing effects and significantly inhibited tumor cell migration. Moreover, the La50 %@FA-CDs showed minimal toxicity to HUVEC and HEK 293 cells. Collectively, these findings suggested that the La50 %@FA-CDs hold promising potential for antitumor applications.

Wenjin Wei: Data curation, Investigation. Qiuhua Jiang: Supervision, Resources. Xinyun Ye: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation. Weilong Huang: Resources. Zhiji Tang: Resources, Investigation. Yuwei Ye: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Lin Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Huanglian Zhong: Methodology, Investigation. Zhuquan Xia: Investigation. Jingtao Huang: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We are submitting our original research paper entitled “ A novel and high-performance tumor inhibitor of La, N co-doped carbon dots for U251 and LN229 cells " for the possible publication in Colloids and Surfaces B: Biointerfaces. All authors have read and approve this version of the article, and due care has been taken to ensure the integrity of the work. I and co-authors state: no part of this paper has been published or submitted elsewhere, and no conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was sponsored by Jiangxi Provincial Health Technology Project; Science and Technology Department of Jiangxi Province (Jiangxi Natural Science Foundation, No. 20224BAB206069, No. 20203BBGL73174, and No. 20244BAB28020); Jiangxi Province High-level and High-skilled Leading Talents Training Project (2021); Ganzhou Science and Technology Plan Project (2023LNS17423 and GZ2024YLJ004).
